feed_card.headline: In the summer of 1945, a Dutch painter sat in an Amsterdam cell, charged with selling a Vermeer to Hermann Göring. A conviction could carry the death penalty.
teasers: Old masters changing hands in wartime
A nation hungry for a lost Vermeer

sections.content: On 29 May 1945, a few weeks after German forces in the Netherlands surrendered, officers came to a grand house on the Keizersgracht in Amsterdam and arrested the man who lived there. In Hermann Göring's recovered art collection they had found a Vermeer, and the trail led to his door. In a country still counting its collaborators, selling a Dutch master to the Reichsmarschall was treason.

content.body: Johannes Vermeer left around thirty-five paintings and almost no record of his life, and the Dutch treated each surviving canvas as a national jewel. For decades scholars had argued over a gap in his story, the early years before the quiet domestic scenes, when a young painter might have tried large religious subjects in the Italian manner. No such works were known. Abraham Bredius, the most authoritative Vermeer scholar of the day, held that they had been lost, and he hoped to see one surface in his lifetime. By the 1930s the art world was primed for exactly such a discovery, and the gap in the catalogue was still open.
content.image_caption: Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring. Fewer than forty paintings by him are known, so each new one was treated as a national event.
content.image_attribution: Johannes Vermeer, "Girl with a Pearl Earring", Public domain (Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: The Painting from Italy
content.body: In 1937 a painting surfaced through a lawyer who said it came from a Dutch family living in Italy. It showed Christ at supper in Emmaus, the moment two travelers recognize the risen Jesus as he breaks the bread. Bredius examined it, set his early doubts aside, and pronounced it genuine. To him it was the lost early Vermeer, the religious work his theory had promised. He published his verdict in The Burlington Magazine, calling it a masterpiece by Vermeer, and the art world accepted the judgment almost without argument. The Rembrandt Society raised the money, and for around 520,000 guilders the Supper at Emmaus went to the Boijmans Museum in Rotterdam.

content.title: A Run of Miracles
content.body: More early Vermeers followed, each in the same religious vein, each backed by the same authority. The Last Supper. Isaac Blessing Jacob. The Head of Christ. Collectors and museums competed for them, and the prices ran into the millions of guilders. The occupation in 1940 did not slow the trade. It grew, and the painter the critics had dismissed for years was now among the wealthiest men in the Netherlands.

content.title: Into Göring's Hands
content.body: Hermann Göring amassed art on an enormous scale, and his agents bought and looted across occupied Europe for his collection. In 1942 one of the newly found Vermeers, Christ with the Adulteress, reached him through a dealer named Alois Miedl. The price was around 1.65 million guilders. Göring hung it among his greatest prizes at Carinhall, his estate outside Berlin, a Vermeer almost no one else had ever seen. The painting had passed from a Dutch studio to the second most powerful man in the Reich.
content.image_caption: Hermann Göring in 1932. His agents bought, pressured, and looted art across occupied Europe for his collection.
content.image_attribution: Unknown photographer, "Hermann Göring" (1932), CC BY-SA 3.0 DE (Bundesarchiv, Bild 102-13805, via Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: The Reckoning
content.body: When the war ended, Allied teams charged with recovering stolen art began pulling masterpieces out of mines and castles across the defeated Reich. Among Göring's hoard they found Christ with the Adulteress. They followed it back through Miedl, and the trail ended at a single name in Amsterdam: Han van Meegeren. He had sold a Dutch national treasure to the enemy, and the new authorities charged him with collaboration. For a crime of that kind, the penalty could be death.

Then van Meegeren broke. The painting he had sold to Göring was no Vermeer at all, he told them. He had painted it himself. The confession went further. The Supper at Emmaus, the celebrated religious discoveries that had thrilled Bredius and filled the museums, all of it had come from his own hand. No one believed him. So he offered to prove it, and under guard, watched by officials and reporters, he set up a canvas where the authorities held him and painted a new Vermeer from nothing. Over the following months Jesus Among the Doctors took shape in front of them.
content.image_caption: Van Meegeren painting Jesus Among the Doctors in 1945, under official watch, to prove the celebrated Vermeers were his own.
content.image_attribution: Koos Raucamp, "Van Meegeren painting Jesus among the Doctors, 1945", Copyrighted free use (Wikimedia Commons)

content.body:
The confession turned his trial inside out. A man accused of betraying his country was now fighting to be believed a swindler instead, because the swindle was the lesser crime. A court-appointed commission led by the Belgian chemist Paul Coremans examined the seized paintings and found the proof in the paint itself, a synthetic resin and a pigment that did not exist in the seventeenth century. The charge of collaboration fell away, and forgery and fraud took its place. By the time the case reached court in 1947, the public had reached its own verdict ahead of the judges. Here was a little-known Dutch painter who had taken more than a million guilders from Hermann Göring and handed him a fake, who had made fools of the experts who had sneered at his own work for years. They loved him for it. The court convicted him and gave him one year. He never served it. On 30 December 1947, before he saw the inside of a prison, his heart gave out, and he died at fifty-eight.

Within a decade the Supper at Emmaus, once the crown of the catalogue, had begun to look like what it was, a face from the 1930s in seventeenth-century dress. Scholars quietly rebuilt the list of real Vermeers. A few of the men who had paid fortunes refused to the end to admit their treasures were nothing of the kind.

sections.content: History remembers van Meegeren because his case shows how expert judgment fails. We picture a great forgery as a perfect copy, a technical feat that fools the eye. His best work was nothing like that. The Supper at Emmaus does not closely resemble any genuine Vermeer. It worked because it answered the question Bredius most wanted answered, the lost religious Vermeer he had spent a career hoping to find, and it arrived in exactly the form he expected. He had not been fooled by a flawless copy. He had been handed his own theory, painted back to him. That is the uncomfortable part of authentication. It runs on expectation, reputation, and the wish to be the one who finds the lost masterpiece, and the deeper an expert's conviction, the larger the target a forger has to aim at. Van Meegeren worked backward from what his audience already hoped was true, which is why a painting that should not have convinced anyone convinced the people best equipped to know.

Parts of the story stay in dispute. The exact number of forgeries van Meegeren made has never been pinned down, and a few paintings still sit in an uncertain zone between his hand and a genuine one. How much the dealers who moved the paintings, and those closest to him, understood about the source of his sudden wealth is unclear. The terms of the Göring sale, how much was cash and how much was traded in other looted art, were tangled even at the time. And one question no document can settle is how many people in the trade quietly thought the discoveries were too good, too convenient, and chose to stay silent while the money was good.

content.question: Why did Abraham Bredius and other experts accept the Supper at Emmaus so readily?
options: It was an exact copy of a Vermeer already hanging in a museum
It matched a long-held theory that Vermeer had painted lost religious works early in his career
Scientific dating had confirmed the canvas came from the 1600s
A signature on it was checked against official records
content.explanation: The painting succeeded because it confirmed the missing religious Vermeer that Bredius had spent his career expecting, not because it copied any known work.

content.question: Why was van Meegeren's situation in 1945 a matter of life and death?
options: He had stolen the painting from a Dutch museum
He had forged official documents for the occupiers
Selling a Dutch national treasure to a Nazi leader was treated as collaboration with the enemy
He had informed on members of the Dutch resistance
content.explanation: He was arrested as a collaborator for handing a Vermeer, a national treasure, to Göring, a crime that could carry the death penalty.

content.question: How did van Meegeren try to prove his confession was true?
options: He produced the original bills of sale
He led investigators to a hidden workshop
He named the experts who had helped him
He painted a new Vermeer from scratch under official supervision
content.explanation: Since no one believed his claim, he demonstrated it by creating a fresh forgery, Jesus Among the Doctors, while officials and reporters watched.

content.question: What allowed his paintings to survive the standard tests for old masters?
options: He bribed the chemists who examined them
He mixed his paint with a synthetic resin and baked it so it hardened like centuries-old paint
He copied existing Vermeers brushstroke by brushstroke
He aged the canvases with smoke from a coal fire
content.explanation: The resin-and-heat method made fresh paint behave like aged paint and resist the solvent test, which is also how a later commission caught the forgery once it detected the modern resin.

content.question: Why does the story treat the experts' own knowledge as the source of their mistake?
options: They had never studied Vermeer closely enough to judge
They relied on machines that gave false readings
The forgery was built to fit the very theory their expertise had produced, so their strongest beliefs worked against them
They were threatened by the occupiers into approving it
content.explanation: Van Meegeren aimed straight at the experts' deepest convictions, so the more invested a scholar was in the lost-Vermeer theory, the more easily he was taken in.

content.question: How did the Dutch public respond once it was clear the paintings were forgeries?
options: They demanded the harshest possible sentence
They turned against the museums that had been fooled
They largely treated van Meegeren as a folk hero who had cheated Göring and embarrassed the critics
They lost interest once the war news returned
content.explanation: The image of a humble Dutch painter swindling the Reichsmarschall and the experts who had dismissed him won wide public sympathy.

content.question: What was the final irony of van Meegeren's fate?
options: The painting he sold Göring turned out to be a real Vermeer after all
Proving he was a forger spared him the traitor's penalty, yet he died before serving even his light forgery sentence
He was pardoned but lost his fortune to lawsuits
Göring was acquitted while van Meegeren was condemned
content.explanation: Confessing to fraud rescued him from a possible death sentence for treason, but a heart attack killed him at the end of 1947 before his one-year term began.

content.name: Han van Meegeren
content.one_line: A scorned Dutch painter who fooled the experts who had dismissed him, then confessed to save his own life.

content.name: Abraham Bredius
content.role: The authority who certified the fake
content.one_line: The leading Vermeer scholar of his day, whose endorsement made the Supper at Emmaus a national treasure.

content.name: Hermann Göring
content.one_line: The Nazi leader whose hunger for masterpieces drew one of the forgeries into his collection.

content.name: Paul Coremans
content.role: The scientist who proved the forgery
content.one_line: The Belgian chemist whose analysis of the paint confirmed the celebrated Vermeers were modern work.

sections.content: The forgeries thrived in a particular moment. The Netherlands of the 1930s revered its Golden Age painters as proof of national greatness, and a lost Vermeer carried weight far beyond the art market. Then came the occupation. From 1940 the Nazi regime ran the largest campaign of art plunder in history, with Göring and Hitler competing to build collections out of the museums, dealers, and Jewish families of occupied Europe. Money flooded the Dutch art trade, and hard questions about where a painting came from were easy to wave away in the confusion of war. After liberation, the country put its collaborators on trial in large numbers, and that machinery is what first caught van Meegeren.

content.label: Edward Dolnick, The Forger's Spell: A True Story of Vermeer, Nazis, and the Greatest Art Hoax of the Twentieth Century (2008)
Wei-lun Toh, A veneer of Vermeer, Chemistry World (2015)
Forgeries of Vermeer paintings, Counting Vermeer (RKD Studies)
